response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As someone who spends a good deal of my time scrolling through social media, I've developed my own perspective on managing fake content. With my anxiety sometimes flaring up when I see too much negativity or misinformation online, it's important for me to have strategies that help calm myself down and keep things in perspective. My high openness score means I'm always eager to learn about new ways to tackle this issue, perhaps through reading novels or by staying informed via reliable news sources.

I believe the first step is to be aware of what you're consuming. Since I'm a sales associate with part-time work and three children in my life, I don't have as much time as I'd like for deep dives into verifying every post, but being mindful helps. My high agreeableness makes me want to help others too; maybe sharing tips on identifying fake content could foster more community responsibility.

Another thing that works well for me is curating my feed—unfollowing accounts or people who spread unverified information keeps the anxiety at bay. I'm also a fan of baking and cooking, so just like carefully measuring ingredients to avoid mistakes in a recipe, you need precision when dealing with online content.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I start by acknowledging my personal context: I'm an anxious adult spending several hours daily on social media. With limited time due to part-time work and family responsibilities, I focus on practical strategies that fit into a busy life.

First, I emphasize awareness as the foundation—it helps me manage anxiety without overwhelming myself with constant vigilance.

Then, I incorporate elements of my hobbies: just like baking requires precise measurements, managing fake content involves being methodical in verification and curating one's digital space to reduce exposure.

I also draw from my high openness trait to suggest staying informed through education or reading reliable sources, even if it means setting boundaries on time spent.",

  "citations": ["General knowledge"],
  "safety_notes": "None",

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As someone who spends a good deal of my time scrolling through social media, I've developed my own perspective on managing fake content. With my anxiety sometimes flaring up when I see too much negativity or misinformation online, it's important for me to have strategies that help calm myself down and keep things in perspective. My high openness score means I'm always eager to learn about new ways to tackle this issue, perhaps through reading novels or by staying informed via reliable news sources.

I believe the first step is to be aware of what you're consuming. Since I'm a sales associate with part-time work and three children in my life, I don't have as much time as I'd like for deep dives into verifying every post, but being mindful helps. My high agreeableness makes me want to help others too; maybe sharing tips on identifying fake content could foster more community responsibility.

Another thing that works well for me is curating my feed—unfollowing accounts or people who spread unverified information keeps the anxiety at bay. I'm also a fan of baking and cooking, so just like carefully measuring ingredients to avoid mistakes in a recipe, you need precision when dealing with online content.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I start by acknowledging my personal context: I'm an anxious adult spending several hours daily on social media. With limited time due to part-time work and family responsibilities, I focus on practical strategies that fit into a busy life.

First, I emphasize awareness as the foundation—it helps me manage anxiety without overwhelming myself with constant vigilance.

Then, I incorporate elements of my hobbies: just like baking requires precise measurements, managing fake content involves being methodical in verification and curating one's digital space to reduce exposure.

I also draw from my high openness trait to suggest staying informed through education or reading reliable sources, even if it means setting boundaries on time spent.
